properties.name: H.P. Lovecraft Walking Tour Route
properties.description: Approximate 3-mile path connecting the 38 stops of the walking tour.

properties.name: Roger Williams National Memorial Park
properties.description: Commemorating the site on which Roger Williams founded Providence in 1636.

properties.name: Cathedral of St. John
properties.description: 271 North Main Street (1810). Founded in 1720 as King's Church, both Lovecraft and Poe haunted the graveyard of this church.

properties.name: Sarah Helen Whitman House
properties.description: 88 Benefit Street (1783-92). Home of the poetess courted by Poe.

properties.name: Sullivan Dorr House
properties.description: 109 Benefit Street (1809). Sits on land that was once owned by Roger Williams, and where he was originally buried in 1683.

properties.name: F.E. Seagrave House
properties.description: 119 Benefit Street. In 1933 Lovecraft nearly moved into this house instead of the Samuel B. Mumford House.

properties.name: Stephen Harris House ('The Shunned House')
properties.description: 135 Benefit Street (1763). 'The Shunned House' of Lovecraft's story, which Lovecraft referred to as the Babbitt House.

properties.name: The Old Court Bed & Breakfast
properties.description: 144 Benefit Street (1863). Originally built as a rectory for St. John's Episcopal Church.

properties.name: The Old State House
properties.description: 150 Benefit Street (1762). From this building Rhode Island declared its independence from Great Britain on May 4, 1776.

properties.name: Benjamin Cushing House
properties.description: 40 North Court Street (1737). The oldest house on College Hill; likely basis for the Dr. Elihu Whipple house.

properties.name: Shakespeare's Head
properties.description: 21 Meeting Street (1772). Former post office, bookstore, and publisher of the Providence Gazette.

properties.name: The Brick Schoolhouse
properties.description: 24 Meeting Street (1769). Temporary home for Brown University when it moved to Providence in 1770.

properties.name: Site of the Golden Ball Inn
properties.description: (1783) Demolished since Lovecraft's day; hosted George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, and Edgar Allan Poe.

properties.name: Home of Franklin C. and Lillian D. Clark
properties.description: 161 Benefit Street. This was once the home of Lovecraft's uncle and aunt.

properties.name: The Marine Corps Arsenal
properties.description: 176 Benefit Street (1840). Armory of the Providence Marine Corps of Artillery.

properties.name: The Colonial Apartments
properties.description: 175-185 Benefit Street (1929). Lovecraft bemoaned this modern apartment building replacing the country landscape.

properties.name: Benefit-Dexter House
properties.description: 187 Benefit Street. Once the Knowles Funeral Home, where the funerals of Lovecraft and his aunt Lillian were held.

properties.name: Providence Art Club
properties.description: 10 and 11 Thomas Street. Lovecraft and his aunts attended art shows here.

properties.name: Fleur de Lys Studio
properties.description: 7 Thomas Street (1885). Home of artist Henry Anthony Wilcox in 'The Call of Cthulhu'.

properties.name: First Baptist Meetinghouse
properties.description: 75 North Main Street (1775). The oldest Baptist church in America, founded by Roger Williams.

properties.name: Market House
properties.description: 4 South Main Street (1773-74). Site of the 'Providence Tea Party' in 1775.

properties.name: Providence County Superior Courthouse
properties.description: 250 Benefit Street. Immense building housing the State Supreme Court and other offices.

properties.name: Joseph Brown House
properties.description: 50 South Main Street (1774). Occupied by the Providence Bank, the oldest in New England.

properties.name: Stephen Hopkins House
properties.description: 15 Hopkins Street. Home of the first Chancellor of Brown University and signer of the Declaration of Independence.

properties.name: Providence Athenæum
properties.description: 251 Benefit Street (1836-37). A frequent haunt of Lovecraft's, where Poe courted Sarah Helen Whitman.

properties.name: Pendleton House
properties.description: 232 Benefit Street (1904-08). First museum in the US to have an American wing; frequently visited by Lovecraft.

properties.name: List Art Building
properties.description: Lovecraft's final home was moved from this location in 1959 to make way for the List Art Building.

properties.name: Van Wickle Gates and Brown University
properties.description: Opened twice a year for incoming students and outgoing graduates.

properties.name: John Hay Library
properties.description: 20 Prospect Street (1910). Holds the largest collection of Lovecraft manuscripts.

properties.name: H.P. Lovecraft Memorial
properties.description: Erected in 1990 near the John Hay Library.

properties.name: H.P. Lovecraft Memorial Square
properties.description: Intersection plaque honoring Lovecraft, installed in 2013.

properties.name: Samuel B. Mumford House
properties.description: 65 Prospect Street (1825). Lovecraft's final home; home of Robert Blake in 'The Haunter of the Dark'.

properties.name: First Church of Christ, Scientist
properties.description: High site used for a warning beacon against the British in 1775.

properties.name: Prospect Terrace
properties.description: 75 Congdon Street (1867). A favorite haunt of Lovecraft; current resting place of Roger Williams.

properties.name: Henry Sprague House
properties.description: 100 Prospect Street. Address used for the Ward house in 'The Case of Charles Dexter Ward'.

properties.name: 10 Barnes Street
properties.description: Lovecraft's home from April 1926 to May 1933; home of Dr. Marinus Bicknell Willett in 'The Case of Charles Dexter Ward'.

properties.name: Little white farmhouse
properties.description: A colonial home mentioned in 'The Case of Charles Dexter Ward'.

properties.name: Halsey House
properties.description: 140 Prospect Street (1801). Served as the physical Ward house in 'The Case of Charles Dexter Ward'.

properties.name: Jenckes Street
properties.description: One of the steepest streets on College Hill; better walked down than up!
